name: What to Do Immediately After Being Struck by a Falling Object at Work
description: What to Do Immediately After Being Struck by a Falling Object at Work - Cottonwood Heights

step.name: Step 1
step.text: Being struck by a falling, flying, or swinging object is one of the "Fatal Four" leading causes of workplace injuries according to OSHA. Whether it was a tool dropped from a scaffold in Salt Lake City or a malfunctioning piece of machinery in Boise, the immediate aftermath of a "struck-by" accident is chaotic. However, the steps you take in the first 24 to 72 hours are critical to securing your workers’ compensation benefits.

step.name: Step 2
step.text: ### What to Do First

step.name: Step 3
step.text: The very first priority is medical stabilization. If you have been hit in the head, chest, or extremities, do not "tough it out." Internal bleeding or concussions may not show symptoms for several hours.

step.name: Step 4
step.text: 1. **Seek Emergency Care:** Go to the nearest emergency room or urgent care. Tell the intake staff clearly: "This happened at work." This ensures the medical record is flagged correctly from the start.

step.name: Step 5
step.text: 2. **Report the Incident to Your Supervisor:** In Utah and Idaho, there are strict timelines for reporting. While you may have up to 30 days in some instances, waiting even a few days can give insurance companies an excuse to claim the injury happened outside of work.

step.name: Step 6
step.text: 3. **Identify Witnesses:** If coworkers saw the object fall or witnessed the impact, get their names. Their testimony is often the difference between a claim being accepted or denied.

step.name: Step 7
step.text: ### How the Process Works

step.name: Step 8
step.text: Once you have reported the injury, your employer should provide you with the necessary forms for the Utah Labor Commission or the Idaho Industrial Commission. Your employer must file an "Employer’s First Report of Injury."

step.name: Step 9
step.text: After the filing, the insurance carrier will investigate. They will look at the type of object, the height it fell from, and whether you were wearing required safety gear like a hard hat. If the claim is accepted, you will begin receiving medical coverage and disability payments (if you are unable to work). If it is denied, the appeals process begins, which involves filing an Application for Hearing.

step.name: Step 10
step.text: ### Key Evidence and Documents

step.name: Step 11
step.text: To build a strong case for a struck-by-object claim, you need more than just a doctor’s note. You should gather:

step.name: Step 12
step.text: - **Photos of the Scene:** Take pictures of the object that hit you, the location where it fell from, and any lack of safety netting or barriers.

step.name: Step 13
step.text: - **Medical Records:** Specifically, the initial ER report that describes the mechanics of the injury.

step.name: Step 14
step.text: - **Equipment Logs:** If a piece of machinery "spat out" an object, logs showing the machine was poorly maintained can be vital evidence.

step.name: Step 15
step.text: - **Wage Statements:** To ensure your disability checks are calculated correctly, keep your recent pay stubs from the weeks leading up to the accident.

step.name: Step 16
step.text: ### Common Mistakes to Avoid

step.name: Step 17
step.text: - **Downplaying Symptoms:** Telling a doctor "I feel okay" when you are actually in pain can be used against you later to terminate benefits prematurely.

step.name: Step 18
step.text: - **Missing Follow-up Appointments:** If you skip physical therapy or doctor visits, the insurance company will argue that your injury has resolved.

step.name: Step 19
step.text: - **Social Media Posts:** Avoid posting photos of yourself being active. Insurance adjusters frequently monitor social media to see if an injured worker is "faking" the severity of their impact injury.

step.name: Step 20
step.text: ### When to Contact a Lawyer

step.name: Step 21
step.text: Most workers can handle a simple, undisputed claim. However, you should contact an attorney at Just Work Comp Law if:

step.name: Step 22
step.text: - Your employer claims the object hit you because you were "horsing around."

step.name: Step 23
step.text: - Your claim is denied because the insurance company says the injury was a pre-existing condition.

step.name: Step 24
step.text: - You have suffered a permanent impairment, such as loss of vision or permanent scarring.

step.name: Step 25
step.text: - Your medical bills are piling up, but the insurance carrier is refusing to pay for a specific surgery or treatment.

step.name: Step 26
step.text: ### Practical Next Steps

step.name: Step 27
step.text: If you've been struck by an object at work today, notify your supervisor in writing (email or text is fine for a paper trail) and go to the doctor. Once you are stable, call Just Work Comp Law at 801-746-0290 for a free consultation to ensure your rights in Utah or Idaho are protected.
